file.content:
# `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus`

The Reckoner — a narrow observer that emits Lattice pulses when a
root writ stalls, fails, or when the guild's work queue drains. It is the
first consumer of the Lattice substrate: a Phase 2 CDC watcher on
`clerk/writs` that emits three trigger types.

See also: [`docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md`](../../../docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md).

---

## Installation

```sh
pnpm add @shardworks/reckoner-apparatus
```

Register the apparatus in `guild.json`:

```json
{
  "plugins": [
    "@shardworks/lattice-apparatus",
    "@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus"
  ]
}
```

The Reckoner has no configuration surface. Install turns it on; uninstall
turns it off.

---

## API

```typescript
interface ReckonerApi {
  readonly source: string;               // always 'reckoner'
  readonly triggerTypes: readonly string[];
}
```

Most callers do not need to touch `ReckonerApi` — the Reckoner runs
autonomously via CDC. The API exists so surfaces (list views, dashboards)
can enumerate the trigger types the Reckoner emits.

### Trigger types

| Trigger | `writId` | Emitted on |
|---|---|---|
| `reckoner.writ-stuck` | root writ id | root writ enters `stuck` and the stuck is terminal non-success. |
| `reckoner.writ-failed` | root writ id | root writ enters `failed`. |
| `reckoner.queue-drained` | `null` | any terminal writ transition that brings the guild to `open = 0 AND active rigs = 0`. |

### Context payloads

Each trigger emits a typed `context` payload on the pulse:

```typescript
interface WritStuckContext {
  writShortId: string;   // `w-abc123`
  writPhase: 'stuck';
  writTitle: string;
  writType: string;
  stuckCause?: string;
  retryable?: boolean;
  detail?: string;
}

interface WritFailedContext {
  writShortId: string;
  writTitle: string;
  writType: string;
  resolution?: string;
  childFailures?: string[];
}

interface QueueDrainedContext {
  drainedAt: string;
  lastTerminalWritId: string;
}
```

Channels (e.g. the Discord kit) use these payloads to render richer
notifications without having to re-parse the pulse's plain-text summary.

---

## Dependencies

- **Required:** `@shardworks/clerk-apparatus`, `@shardworks/lattice-apparatus`, `@shardworks/stacks-apparatus`.
- **Recommended:** `@shardworks/clockworks-retry-apparatus` — the Reckoner reads
  `maxAttempts` from the retry apparatus's API to decide whether a stuck is
  terminal. When clockworks-retry is absent, every stuck is terminal from
  the Reckoner's viewpoint.
- **Recommended:** `@shardworks/spider-apparatus` — the Reckoner reads
  `spider/rigs` for retry-cap evaluation and drain counts. Without Spider
  the rig counts resolve to zero and the Reckoner still emits
  correctly-shaped pulses.

---

## Behavior

- **Roots-only.** Child-writ transitions never emit their own pulses; the
  Clerk's cascade rewrites the parent's resolution with the child's cause,
  and the Reckoner surfaces leaf failures in the parent pulse's
  `childFailures` context field.
- **Phase 2 CDC.** The observer runs post-commit with `failOnError: false`:
  a pulse failure never voids the underlying writ transition.
- **No startup backfill.** The Reckoner fires on transitions only.
  Restarting a guild with pre-existing stuck / failed writs does not
  produce pulses for them.
- **No dedupe on drain.** Multiple terminal transitions in rapid
  succession may each emit a drain pulse. This is an accepted MVP cost.

---

## Support Kit

None — the Reckoner contributes no books, no tools, and no pages. Its
only observable effect is the pulses it writes to the Lattice.

lastPrompt: have we implemented any logic for the Reckoner yet?

content.text:
Yep — the Reckoner is fully implemented and lives at `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/reckoner/`. It's in pretty good shape:

**What's there:**
- `reckoner.ts` (259 lines) — the actual apparatus: a Phase 2 CDC watcher on `clerk/writs` that emits three Lattice trigger types.
- `predicates.ts` — `isTerminalStuck`, `parseChildFailures`, `writShortId` helpers.
- `drain.ts` — `isQueueDrained` predicate (open writs + active rigs both zero).
- `types.ts` — `ReckonerApi`, the three trigger-type constants, and the `WritStuckContext` / `WritFailedContext` / `QueueDrainedContext` payload shapes.
- Full test coverage: `reckoner.test.ts` (455 lines), `drain.test.ts`, `predicates.test.ts`, plus a 545-line `integration.test.ts`.
- A 122-line README documenting the whole surface.

**The three trigger types it emits:**
| Trigger | When |
|---|---|
| `reckoner.writ-stuck` | root writ enters `stuck` and the stuck is terminal non-success |
| `reckoner.writ-failed` | root writ enters `failed` |
| `reckoner.queue-drained` | any terminal transition brings `open = 0 AND active rigs = 0` |

**Shape of it:** Roots-only (child failures surface via the parent's cascaded resolution in `childFailures`), no startup backfill, `failOnError: false` so pulse failures don't void the underlying writ transition. Soft-deps on `clockworks-retry` (for `maxAttempts`) and `spider` (for the `rigs` book); degrades gracefully if either is absent.

Want me to pull up anything specific — the tests, the integration test scenarios, or the docs at `docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md`?

content.text:
Nope — nothing yet on the auto-commission side. The Reckoner is purely the observer half: it emits pulses into the Lattice and stops there. Nothing currently subscribes to those pulses to turn them into new commissions.

**What the Reckoner does NOT do:**
- No standing-order registration that maps `reckoner.writ-stuck` → "dispatch a fix commission."
- No petition / self-commission flow hanging off it.
- No overseer process consuming its triggers.
- The only mention of "overseer" in the whole plugin is a single comment in `astrolabe/plan-and-ship.ts` about a future curator ("human or overseer") promoting briefs.

**Where the auto-commission design lives (all still clicks, no code):**
- *Stage 3: Self-commissioning* — 7 live children, 0 code artifacts.
  - *Design the overseer pattern: a standing process that observes system state and creates work without patron prompting*
  - *Design the vision-keeper role — the agent/apparatus that holds a forward-looking product vision and decomposes it into commissionable work*
  - *Design how the system surfaces and performs its own improvements*
  - *Design first-class internal commission flow (Petition)*
  - *Ship MVP Overseer: `nsg overseer commission`* — this is the closest to actionable; it's under the overseer-pattern click with the other two "rule-based vs LLM-observer" and "measure duplication" children.

So the current state is: **signal-emission side shipped, consumption side still entirely in the design-clicks stage.** The Lattice pulses are currently consumed only by the Discord channel (for human notification) — nothing programmatic reads them to commission work.

Want to dig into any of those design threads? The overseer-pattern one (`c-moaj06ty`) is probably the best entry point — its MVP child is the most concrete proposal we've got.

content: You asked whether any auto-commission logic is built on top of the Reckoner. Answer: no — the Reckoner only emits Lattice pulses; the self-commissioning side (overseer, petition, vision-keeper) is still entirely in design clicks. Next: pick a thread to dig into. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
- **Petition (internal-source variant)** → first-class internal commissions (`w-mnszzo8h-42137bda6681`) — under umbrella `w-mo0e2m9q` *Unlocking autonomous operation*. The petition concept itself is broader (any formally submitted request, patron or internal); this bookmark tracks the internal-source flow specifically.
- **Vigil** → reserved as the label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions. Earlier bookmark scope (background monitoring of in-flight commissions — click `c-mo1mqgf9`, writ `w-mnszzon4-731bd9827d05`) is now one Reckoner function among several (vision-keeper `c-moa42rxh`, overseer `c-moaj06ty`, in-flight monitoring `c-mo1mqgf9`, intervention pulses `c-mo1z3teo`).

Other terms in this tome remain latent — they live here until they earn a bookmark or get absorbed into an active inquiry.

## Roles (not yet foundational)

--

Pulses are used for intervention signals (Reckoner → patron), status reports, completion notices, and any other guild-internal or guild-to-patron transmission. Trigger-type on the pulse carries urgency — the pulse itself is a neutral container.

Punchier successor to the earlier "missive" framing; coheres better with the Lattice-as-network metaphor. The pulse is the *transmission event*, which cleanly decouples it from payload size — short signals and payload-bearing completion reports both fit.

## Apparatuses

--

The guild's general-purpose messaging apparatus. Carries pulses between any two points — agent to agent, guild to patron, apparatus to apparatus. Delivers directly (no intermediate courier; the earlier "Herald" carrier role was dropped when this shape settled). Any part of the guild can emit a pulse through the Lattice: the Reckoner (intervention signals), Coinmaster (budget warnings), Clerk (approvals needed), Artificers (completion reports), etc.

System mapping: outbox + delivery infrastructure for notifications and signals. Pulses are immutable records (durable event log, not mutable tasks) stored in the book; the Lattice reads pending-delivery pulses and ships them through configured channels. Pulses have no acknowledgment state — their referent (typically a writ) holds the live state, and "still needs attention" is derived by joining to that referent.

### The Reckoner

The guild's command-and-control apparatus — the executive function that senses guild state and acts on it. Responsibilities:

- **Monitor the patron's vision** — the strategic direction the guild is meant to advance.
- **Monitor operational parameters** — cost, quality, queue state, daemon health, commissions in flight, drift from expected behavior.
--

Subsumes several existing design clicks as internal functions: c-moa42rxh (vision-keeper), c-moaj06ty (overseer pattern), c-mo1mqgf9 (background monitoring of in-flight commissions), c-mo1z3teo (intervention pulses). Specific internal decomposition (what watchers, what contracts) still to be designed.

## Records & History

### Stories (working term)

--

**Vigil** — the *activity* of watching and waiting, not an actor or construct. Vigil is what is *kept* — the ongoing monitoring itself. Now reserved as a label for the Reckoner's oversight/maintenance/watching functions: the Reckoner keeps vigil over guild operational parameters, in-flight commissions, and the patron's vision. Specific implementations — what exactly is watched, how, when, by what sub-construct — are still to be designed. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet.

**Sentinel** — reserved term for a single scoped watcher function within the Reckoner. A Sentinel watches one specific thing (writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balance, drift from vision) and emits a pulse into the Lattice when its condition fires. Plural by design: the Reckoner can host many Sentinels, each with a narrow scope, composed together into the Reckoner's overall vigil. Not introduced to code or architecture docs yet — reserved until the Reckoner's internal decomposition is firmed up.

**Rite of Naming** — the ceremony by which a new anima is called into being and given their identity. Not just "add to database" — the naming is when the spirit receives its name and seal, becoming a distinct presence in the guild. The rite could include an initial training session at the Academy (aspirant phase), a trial by craft, and formal induction to the roster as an active anima.

**Requiem** — the process of retiring an anima. Not dissolution — the anima's record persists in the register forever, their seals remain on their work, their lore is preserved. But the spirit passes to rest: `retired` state, no longer manifested. System mapping: a formal process that includes archiving the anima's current instructions and recording why they were retired.

content.text:
## c-moaj06ty — Overseer Pattern

**Goal:** *"Design the overseer pattern: a standing process that observes system state and creates work without patron prompting."*

Some real scoping happened under this umbrella. The subtree tells a story:

**Shipped (concluded):**
- *Astrolabe changes: structured `PlanDoc.observations` + post-planning step that creates draft writs per observation.* — Live as of 2026-04-23 (commission `w-moaz6k39`, sealed `ad65df3c`). `PlanDoc.observations` is now a structured array (`{id, title, body}`), and one brief-type writ in `new` status is created per observation, parented under the originating brief. **This is the input side of the pipeline.**

**Still live (consumer side):**
- *Ship MVP Overseer: `nsg overseer commission` — promote one draft writ from the new pool to open.* (c-moaz1q9b)
- *Rule-based vs. LLM-observer vs. hybrid: what is the right evaluation shape?* (c-moaj0a7u)
- *Measure observation-draft duplication post-hoc via cancelled-writ and small-diff analysis.* (c-moaz1r58)

**Dropped (interesting archaeology):**
- Three earlier scopes got superseded as the design sharpened: the clicks-substrate route, a broader brief-authoring overseer, and the old "dropped-click analysis" methodology. Each was replaced with a narrower version — the team found that once observations-as-draft-mandates was locked in, the overseer's job shrank to pure curation (promote `new` → `open`), with no brief authorship and no click-tree reading.

## c-moa42rxh — Vision-keeper

**Goal:** *"Design the vision-keeper role — the agent/apparatus that holds a forward-looking product vision and decomposes it into commissionable work."*

Nothing else. **Zero children, no notes, no conclusion.** Created 2026-04-22, untouched since. Just the bare goal string.

## Also worth knowing — naming

Both of these clicks have been folded into a larger umbrella concept. Per `vocabulary-aliases.yaml` and the vocabulary tome (updated 2026-04-23):

> **Reckoner** — the guild's command-and-control apparatus, the executive function that senses guild state and acts on it. Subsumes: vision-keeper (c-moa42rxh), overseer pattern (c-moaj06ty), background monitoring of in-flight commissions (c-mo1mqgf9), intervention pulses (c-mo1z3teo).

So "Overseer" legacy → "Reckoner" canonical. The existing plugin at `packages/plugins/reckoner/` is a *narrow* realization under that broader name — it's one function of the future Reckoner (the pulse-emission piece for writ-stuck/failed/drained), not the whole thing. The vision-keeping, in-flight monitoring, and auto-commissioning pieces are all still design-stage.

Reserved vocabulary waiting for design:
- **Vigil** — the ongoing *activity* of watching (not a construct).
- **Sentinel** — a single scoped watcher within the Reckoner (e.g. writ state, queue depth, daemon health, purse balance, drift from vision). Plural by design.

Neither term has landed in code or architecture docs yet.

message.content: i'd like to work on the reckoner's visionining capability. why dont you start by brainstorming what you think that means, then ill correct and gap-fill

content.text:
Okay, here goes — brainstorming unfiltered, then you correct.

## What "visioning" means to me

The word itself is doing a lot of work. I read it as a bundle of related capabilities, not one thing:

**1. Holding** — representing the patron's forward-looking intent in a form the guild can read. Not just "a doc" — some durable, queryable state that survives Sean's attention drifting elsewhere. This is the *memory* aspect.

**2. Refreshing** — updating that representation as Sean's thinking evolves, without requiring him to rewrite a vision doc every time he changes his mind in conversation. Passive absorption from ongoing patron activity is the ideal; periodic patron-ratified rewrites is the fallback.

**3. Projecting** — applying the held vision to a specific situation. "Is commission X aligned with our direction?" "Does this line of inquiry still matter?" "What would a good outcome for this rig look like, given where we're headed?" The vision isn't useful unless other agents can ask it questions.

**4. Gap-spotting** — comparing desired state to current state. Where is there daylight between *where we said we're going* and *what the guild is actually doing right now*? This is where vision crosses into auto-commissioning.

**5. Decomposing** — turning a big gap into small-enough chunks that each is commissionable. "Build the Reckoner" is not commissionable. "Add a Sentinel that watches purse balance" is. The vision-keeper needs to carry the ladder between those.

## What gets *held* (the artifact question)

Candidates, roughly ordered from dumbest to cleverest:

- **Static vision doc** — Sean-authored `vision.md`. The Reckoner reads it. Cheap, legible, but stale the moment it's written.
- **Structured vision graph** — goals, sub-goals, constraints, non-goals, priority, deadlines. Typed, queryable. Costs authoring overhead; pays back in every downstream query.
- **Inferred-from-activity vision** — vision *is* whatever clicks are live, whatever experiments are active, whatever commissions are in flight. No separate artifact. Risk: what you're *currently doing* isn't always what you *want to be doing*. Misses aspirational direction.
- **Patron-commitments ledger** — first-class records of "Sean said X, not Y" decisions, extracted from transcripts or filed explicitly. Chronologically ordered, source-traceable. Newest non-retracted commitment wins.
- **Layered mix** — a small patron-authored core (philosophy, product vision) + a living commitments ledger + derived views over clicks/experiments for "what's active right now." Each layer has clear authorship and freshness properties.

I suspect the real answer is the layered mix — no single artifact is going to carry both "we're building Nexus Mk 2.1 to explore AI-enabled development" *and* "right now Sean cares about unblocking the hopper."

## What gets *produced* (the output question)

If visioning is an active capability, what comes out of it?

- **Queries answered** — other apparatuses ask questions; vision-keeper responds. "Is this aligned?" "What's the priority ordering?" "Is this line still alive?"
- **Draft work items** — when a gap is spotted, a brief/writ/mandate gets proposed (probably as a `new`-status writ, slotting into the existing observations-to-drafts pipeline). This is where visioning becomes auto-commissioning.
- **Alignment pulses** — Lattice emissions when something's off: "no active work advances any vision line," "recent commissions all cluster on a dropped initiative," "patron vision appears to have drifted but no artifact captures the change."
- **Summaries for the patron** — "here's what I think you currently want; correct me." A periodic reflection surface, not an interrupt.

## What's hard (the tensions I'd want to work through)

- **Tacit vs explicit.** Most of Sean's vision lives in his head and is revealed conversationally. A vision-keeper that requires Sean to explicitly author state will stay stale; one that infers from conversation is ambitious and fragile.
- **Scope layering.** Strategic vision ("explore AI-enabled development") vs product vision ("autonomous agent framework") vs tactical vision ("close the planner-implementer fidelity gap"). Is the Reckoner's visioning one of these, all three, or does it distinguish them? Mixing layers gives you incoherent gap analysis.
- **Vision vs reality reconciliation.** Budget, capacity, current-state constraints. A vision-keeper that only reasons about desired state will propose commissions the guild can't afford or doesn't have capability for. Where does that reconciliation happen — inside the visioning, or downstream?
- **Retractability.** Sean changes his mind. Vision from last week may be stale this week. The held state needs a clean retraction/supersession model or it calcifies.
- **Overlap with humans.** Sean already does visioning in his head and in conversation. The vision-keeper is either (a) capturing his visioning for later use, (b) doing independent visioning the patron reviews, or (c) both. These are very different system shapes.
- **Overlap with other Reckoner functions.** "No active work advances vision" is indistinguishable from "queue is drained in a structurally bad way" — one is a vision signal, one is a Sentinel reading. Clean separation may not exist.

## Rough MVP shape I'd propose

If I had to pick a v0 that's small enough to actually ship but interesting enough to teach us something:

1. **A patron-authored vision artifact** — probably a short, structured `vision.md` (or vision.yaml with titled sections). Not the whole answer, but the anchor.
2. **A read interface** — something like `guild().apparatus('reckoner').vision()` that returns the parsed vision for other apparatuses to query.
3. **One query function** — "given this brief/commission/writ title, does it advance any vision line?" Simple keyword/embedding match to start, Claude-evaluated later.
4. **One pulse** — emit `reckoner.vision-drift` when no commission in the last N days has touched any vision line. Don't auto-commission yet; just flag.
5. **Deliberately skip, for v0** — conversation-inference, auto-drafting writs, prioritization reasoning, multi-layer vision. Those are v1+.

That gives us a scaffolded Reckoner capability that's real but doesn't try to be clever yet. Once we see how the artifact-and-query side behaves, we've got data for designing the generative side.

---

Where I'm weakest: I'm guessing at the *layering* question (strategic/product/tactical) and the *conversation-inference* question without much grounding. I also don't have a clear picture of how vision-keeping plays with the existing commission log and experiment index — those already carry vision-ish information and I don't know if the vision-keeper should subsume/read/ignore them.

Your turn — correct and gap-fill.

message.content: the visioning work is where i want to shift from 'building nexus mk 2.1' to 'using nexus to build otehr things'. This makes it a critical juncture to start considering 'multi-codex guilds'. Right now our guild only has one codex, the nexus repo itself. But I want the guild to be able to build other things, like 'hotel-website-xyz'. Each codex (or group of codexes?) will potentially ahve its own vision

content.text:
Ohhh — that recontextualizes basically everything. Let me re-scope.

## What this changes

The visioning capability isn't "hold Nexus's vision" — it's **hold whatever vision(s) belong to whatever codex(es) this guild is currently working on.** Nexus is just the first customer. The vision-keeper needs to be plural-by-construction, or we'll bake single-codex assumptions in and have to tear them out later.

That means several of my earlier assumptions need retuning:

- **Vision isn't singular.** The held state is a set, keyed by codex (or codex-group).
- **Gap analysis is scoped.** "No active work advances vision" is meaningless without "whose vision." Every projection/query needs a codex context.
- **The guild itself may or may not have a vision.** Is there a guild-level direction ("this quarter we're focused on hotel-website-xyz and deprioritizing infrastructure work"), or is guild-level behavior derived from the aggregated codex visions plus patron priority?

## Codex as a unit — things I need to understand better

I know `packages/plugins/codexes/` exists but I haven't studied it. Some things the design hinges on:

1. **Codex registration.** How does a codex join a guild today? Config entry? Init command? Does the guild *own* the codex, or does it merely have read/write access to a workspace that happens to hold one?
2. **Grouping.** You floated "group of codexes" — is that already a concept, or is it aspirational? A microservices shop with a platform repo, a client SDK, and three services might want *one* vision across all five. Or five linked-but-distinct visions. That's a modeling fork.
3. **Authority.** For Nexus, you're the patron and the codex author. For hotel-website-xyz, are you still both, or does the codex have its own client whose intent needs capturing? (I can see this going two ways — guild-of-one-patron with multiple codexes vs. guild-as-service-provider with multiple stakeholder voices.)
4. **Vision location.** Two clean options: (a) vision lives *in the codex* (a known file, travels with the repo, survives guild reinstalls); (b) vision lives *in the sanctum* (cross-codex legibility, patron-curated, but the codex loses its intent when detached). I lean toward (a)-with-cache: canonical source in the codex, Reckoner reads it on registration and on change, mirrors into its own state for fast queries.

## What visioning looks like in a multi-codex world

Revising the capability decomposition:

**1. Per-codex holding.** One vision artifact per codex (or codex-group), discovered via some registration mechanism. Structured enough that gap-spotting and alignment queries can operate on it, but simple enough that a patron can author it by hand.

**2. Cross-codex portfolio view.** The Reckoner — or something sitting just above the per-codex vision-keepers — holds a *portfolio* layer: which codexes are active, which are paused, what's the current priority ordering, what are the cross-codex dependencies. This is where "hotel-website-xyz is the priority this month" lives. Separate from any single codex's vision.

**3. Scoped projection.** Alignment queries are always (vision, work-item) pairs where the vision is explicit. No implicit "the vision." The commission/brief/writ needs to name which codex it targets (it probably already does — this is a check-rather-than-change).

**4. Scoped gap-spotting.** "No active work advances codex X's vision for N days" is a sensible pulse. "Commissions targeting codex X have drifted from its vision" is sensible. "The guild is doing nothing" is also sensible, but it's a portfolio-level pulse, not a vision-level one.

**5. Codex onboarding as a first-class flow.** Adding hotel-website-xyz to the guild should probably include a "capture the initial vision" step — patron writes it, Reckoner ingests it, becomes queryable. This is arguably the *most important* flow, because it's where visioning gets bootstrapped for a new customer.

## Portfolio vs per-codex — the key structural question

I think there's a clean split that might be useful:

| Layer | Lives where | What it answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Codex vision** | With the codex | "What is this codex for? What's the desired state?" |
| **Portfolio** | Guild/sanctum level | "Which codexes get attention, and when?" |
| **Guild operational vision** | Guild level | "How does this guild operate? What's its quality bar, rhythm, standards?" |

The Reckoner's visioning capability is probably *all three*, but they're different artifacts with different authorship and different refresh cadences. Codex visions change slowly (product-level). Portfolio shifts more often (weekly-ish). Guild operational vision is near-static.

If we conflate them, gap-spotting gets muddy ("no commissions this week" — was that a portfolio choice or a vision drift?). Separating them gives us clean predicates.

## Revised MVP shape

Scrapping my earlier single-doc MVP. New v0:

1. **Codex-vision protocol** — a known file in each codex repo (e.g. `.guild/vision.md` or `VISION.md` at root) with a defined structure. Reckoner reads this on codex registration and watches for changes.
2. **Portfolio artifact** — a sanctum-level `portfolio.yaml` (or similar) listing registered codexes, their priority ordering, active/paused state.
3. **Read interfaces** — `reckoner.visionFor(codexId)` and `reckoner.portfolio()`.
4. **One alignment check** — given a brief/commission and a codex id, return a coarse "advances-vision / orthogonal / conflicts" judgment. Dumb to start (keywords), smarter later.
5. **Two pulses** — `reckoner.vision-drift` (per codex) and `reckoner.portfolio-idle` (guild-level).
6. **Deliberately skip** — auto-drafting writs, grouping multiple codexes under shared vision, conversation-inference, cross-codex dependency reasoning, multi-stakeholder vision.

This gives us the shape without the ambition, and it's genuinely exercised by the pivot: you'll add hotel-website-xyz as the second codex, and the system has to behave sensibly.

## Open questions I'd want your answers on

1. Does a **codex** (as already implemented) have its own git repo, or is it a subdirectory of a guild-owned repo, or something else?
2. Is there a **codex-group** concept yet, or is that aspirational? Do we need it for v0?
3. For hotel-website-xyz, who is the **vision author** — you, a client, or you acting as a proxy for a client?
4. Is there already a notion of **portfolio priority** anywhere, or do I invent it fresh?
5. Should **Nexus itself** (as a codex) get the vision-file treatment in this v0, or do we leave the Nexus codex un-visioned until the machinery settles with a greenfield codex first?

And the one I'm most curious about: is the vision-keeper the thing that *authors* the initial vision through a patron interview, or the thing that *holds* a patron-authored vision? Those are very different system shapes, and I can see arguments for both.

content: We're designing the Reckoner's visioning capability, now reframed as multi-codex since you want the guild to build things beyond Nexus itself. Next: you answer the five open questions, starting with how codexes are registered today. (disable recaps in /config)

(A product may have many microservice codexes, a codex may be a shared library used across prdocuts, etc.) I do want to be able to capture relative priorities for 'products' your 'portfolio vision' so that the reckoner can make good decisions. I think we need to make sure and consider the decomposition, as well... I don't want the reckoner to just create a writ taht says "Implement this vision"

content.text:
Okay, big restructure. Products become the pivot, codexes become implementation detail, portfolio becomes the scheduling layer. Let me re-walk it.

## Revised conceptual model

| Concept | Exists? | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| **Patron** | Yes (implicit) | 1:N products |
| **Portfolio** | No — new | 1:1 patron, ordered collection of products |
| **Product** | No — new | 1:1 with vision |
| **Product vision** | No — new | 1:1 with product |
| **Codex** | Yes | M:N with products (via something — see below) |
| **Commission / writ** | Yes | Targets one or more codexes in service of a product's vision |

The M:N between products and codexes probably needs a join concept — "this codex contributes to these products" or "this product is built from these codexes." Could be a flat list in each direction, could be typed (e.g. "primary codex" vs "shared library dependency"). I'd keep it as simple bidirectional tags for v0 and see how the edge cases pile up.

## The decomposition problem

This is the meat. Your "don't let it just say 'implement this vision'" is the whole design constraint. Let me unpack what that demands.

**The claim:** between a product vision and a commissionable writ, there is *real, non-automatable thinking work* that produces a ladder of progressively concrete units. The Reckoner must own the *state* of that ladder, coordinate the *process* of extending it, but never shortcut the thinking itself.

### What a decomposition ladder looks like

Working vocabulary (don't let me name these — they're placeholders):

1. **Vision** — narrative, aspirational, human-authored. Near-static.
2. **Capability** — a coarse chunk of the vision. "Users can book rooms," "operators can manage inventory," "payments work end-to-end." Maybe 5-15 per product. Patron-authored or Reckoner-proposed + patron-ratified.
3. **Outcome / initiative** — a concrete achievable step toward a capability. "Stripe integration live in sandbox mode," "room-browsing UI supports filter-by-amenity." Multiple per capability. Mix of patron-authored and agent-proposed.
4. **Commission / brief** — actually-dispatchable work. Generated from an outcome just-in-time, via the existing brief pipeline.

Each level has:
- An **importance** (relative to its parent — "how much does this capability advance the vision" / "how much does this outcome advance the capability")
- A **status** (proposed, ratified, in-progress, complete, dropped)
- **Provenance** (who added it — patron / agent / shipped-work-observation)

Importance composes down the tree: a writ's effective priority = `product_importance × capability_importance × outcome_importance × leaf_importance`. Or kept as a vector and combined only at scheduling time. I'd keep it as a vector (composition function is a scheduling policy decision, not a data model one — easy to change later).

### Where does decomposition come from?

Three sources feed the ladder:

- **Top-down (patron-initiated)**: the patron sketches vision → capabilities → outcomes. Coarse but explicit. This is the initial population and periodic refresh.
- **Bottom-up (work-initiated)**: already-shipped pipeline — `PlanDoc.observations` → draft writs. In the new model these become *proposed ladder nodes* (usually outcomes, sometimes capabilities) that need parenting into the existing ladder. The Reckoner's job is to catch them and route them to the right place.
- **Reckoner-initiated**: the Reckoner notices something the ladder is missing and proposes a node. E.g., "the vision mentions GDPR but no capability covers it" — emits a `reckoner.capability-gap` proposal.

All three converge on the same state; provenance is tracked per node. Patron ratification is the common gate before a node becomes "live" for scheduling.

### Where decomposition *lives* (the state question)

Options:
- **In the product record** — a structured document per product, like a nested YAML.
- **In the click tree** — reuse the existing Ratchet apparatus; the ladder becomes a specialized shape of clicks.
- **In a new book** — first-class entries with types (`capability`, `outcome`) and a parent pointer.
- **In writs** — writs already have types and parent pointers; make capabilities and outcomes just more writ types.

I lean **new book with typed entries**, because:
- Click tree is the reasoning domain, not the intent domain — overloading it muddies the Ratchet's purpose.
- Writs are execution obligations — making vision-ladder nodes into writs means the lifecycle (new → open → stuck → completed) gets weird. A capability doesn't really "complete" the way a commission does.
- A new book is cheap (the guild already has Stacks) and lets the vocabulary stay crisp.

But this is a modeling call where I'd defer to your judgment — the "everything is a writ" direction is also coherent and has fewer moving parts.

### What the Reckoner actually does with the ladder

Runtime behaviors (layered, roughly MVP-first):

1. **Hold the ladder** — persist, query, version.
2. **Propagate priority** — compute effective priority for any leaf on demand.
3. **Schedule** — select next commission(s) from the leaf set based on priority, capacity, patron-presence, budget.
4. **Spot gaps** — emit pulses for visions without capabilities, capabilities without outcomes, capabilities that claim completion but have no shipped work, outcomes that are stale.
5. **Propose nodes** — generate candidate decomposition nodes for patron review (the active-inference move).
6. **Reconcile drift** — when vision shifts and a capability is no longer aligned, flag for review rather than silently invalidating work.

(1)-(3) are the MVP core. (4)-(6) are progressively smarter.

## Priority and scheduling

Your tuple: `{product_importance, task_importance_to_product}`. With the ladder, this expands to a vector:

`{product_importance, capability_importance, outcome_importance, leaf_importance}`

Scheduling policy combines them. Simplest: multiply for a single scalar, schedule by rank. Richer: keep as vector, apply different weights at different times (e.g. when patron is present, weight product_importance more; when autonomous, weight throughput-friendly outcomes more). The tuple-vs-scalar question is a policy knob — I'd store as vector, let policy decide.

Some priority dynamics worth designing in from the start:
- **Reassessment cadence** — priorities should be treated as mutable state with a "last set" timestamp. Policy can decay confidence over time (a 6-month-old capability importance probably deserves a re-look).
- **Patron-override channel** — any priority can be pinned by the patron, which prevents agent-proposed recomputation.
- **Cross-product fairness** — do we guarantee *some* work happens on every active product, or are we okay with the whole guild focusing on one product for a month? (Policy question.)

## What I'd propose for MVP

1. **Introduce `product` and `portfolio` as first-class records.** Portfolio is a sanctum-level artifact listing products and their relative importance. Product is a directory (or record) with: name, vision narrative, codex associations, and a vision ladder.
2. **Introduce a vision ladder data shape** — typed nodes (capability, outcome), parent pointers, importance field, status, provenance. New Stacks book, probably `reckoner/vision-nodes` or similar.
3. **Introduce the read API** — `reckoner.portfolio()`, `reckoner.product(id)`, `reckoner.ladder(productId)`, `reckoner.scheduledLeaves()`.
4. **One authorship flow** — patron-authored initial vision + capabilities + outcomes via some guided interface (could be as simple as editing a file; could be a `nsg reckoner elicit` CLI that walks the patron through it).
5. **One scheduling behavior** — when a leaf is ratified and no blocking commission is running for that product, the Reckoner drops a draft writ into the existing new-status pool (reusing the observations-to-drafts plumbing). No auto-dispatch yet — just a draft.
6. **One gap pulse** — `reckoner.vision-node-missing` when a capability has no outcomes, a product has no capabilities, or the portfolio has no products. Deliberately boring.
7. **Deliberately skip** — Reckoner-proposed nodes, priority decay, cross-product fairness policies, auto-dispatch, multi-stakeholder vision.

This gives you enough to stand up a second product (hotel-website-xyz) as a real test case and actually see the whole flow work: author vision → ladder gets populated → leaf gets drafted as a writ → existing pipeline takes over.

## Open questions for you

1. **Ladder state location** — new book, clicks, writs, or something else? I leaned toward new book; you have more context on whether that's the right call.
2. **Decomposition ownership** — is the patron authoring all three levels (vision, capabilities, outcomes), or is some part of this Reckoner-proposed-with-patron-review from day one?
3. **Priority storage** — vector or scalar? I'd store vector, combine at schedule time.
4. **Patron-as-product-proxy** — you said this detail can be hidden from the guild. Confirming: the guild doesn't need to model "this product serves external client X"? Just "patron says product Y has importance 8"?
5. **Nexus itself** — does Nexus become a first-class product-with-vision in this model, or does it stay in its current weird self-referential state? (I'd argue: make it a product with a vision, and use it to dogfood the machinery.)
6. **Does the ladder need dependency edges?** — "outcome A depends on outcome B across products." I'd defer this, but the data model should leave room for it.

Ready for your next pass.

lastPrompt: thinking about it more, i'd like to decouple 'visions' from 'codexes'. The portfolio idea is really the right one.. there is a 1:1 relationship between "products" and "visions", but there is not a 1:1…

message.content: considering 'ladder-as-writs'... this is the 2nd or 3rd time now where we have wanted to use 'writs' to capture something, but shied away because of the "obligation" framing and lifecycle weirdness. I'm wondering, now, if instead of continuing to build new 'things' we should look at expanding/changing our writ structure to be more flexible? Again, 'writs' dont have to be units of obligations... that can be relegated to a single writ type, 'mandate'. That just leaves the lifecycle... I see two possibities. (1) change the writ lifecycles to be something more general that maps more cleanly to other writ types, or (2) make the lifecycle configure, with different writs having their own lifecylce definitions. Thoughts on these?

content: to you other questsion... (4) correct; (5) yes, i say dogfood; (6) agree with defer-but-allow-for-it

content.text:
Strong agreement on the smell. Every time we invent a new first-class record type, we're making the cross-cutting infrastructure (Clockworks, Stacks watchers, the Reckoner's drain detection, the Ratchet-for-clicks, the-thing-we'd-need-for-ladders) do duplicated work. Clicks-as-parallel-system was already an accident of timing — writs didn't have the right shape, so Ratchet was built. Doing it again for products/capabilities/outcomes compounds that mistake.

Let me work through your two options, then propose a third.

## Option 1 — unified general lifecycle

The attempt: find a lifecycle that maps cleanly to every type. Something abstract like `draft → active → paused → resolved → abandoned`.

**Problem:** either the states get so abstract they're unhelpful ("what does `active` mean for a capability that no one 'works on' — just that it's been ratified?"), or type-specific concerns bleed into the shared vocabulary and the abstraction leaks.

`stuck` is the clearest example. It's *essential* for mandates (the guild actually gets stuck) but meaningless for capabilities or clicks. Either it stays in the general lifecycle and most types ignore it (confusing), or it disappears and mandates lose a semantic distinction that matters (worse).

Shared lifecycles also tend to force false equivalences — a "completed capability" and a "completed mandate" are doing very different work, and papering over that with one word is going to produce subtle bugs in every downstream consumer.

**Verdict:** tempting because it's simple, but I don't think it survives the semantic diversity of the types we're already contemplating.

## Option 2 — per-type configurable lifecycles

Each writ type declares its own state machine. Mandate keeps what it has. Capability gets `proposed → ratified → shipped/retired`. Click gets `live → parked → concluded/dropped`. Etc.

**Pros:**
- Each type gets exactly the states it needs — names are semantically honest.
- Additive migration — no breaking changes to mandate.
- The Clerk becomes a generic state-machine engine, which is what it should be.
- Makes "add a new writ type" a config change, not a new subsystem.

**Cons:**
- Cross-cutting infrastructure breaks. The Reckoner's drain detector says "open count = 0 AND active rigs = 0" — that's currently generic across all writs, but in a world of type-specific states, `open` isn't a word every type uses.
- Observers that want to react to "any terminal transition" can't know what "terminal" means without per-type knowledge.
- Risk of lifecycle sprawl — every new type invents its own names, and we end up with `ratified`, `open`, `confirmed`, `accepted` all meaning basically the same thing.

**The infrastructure problem is the big one.** Raw Option 2 makes every cross-cutting apparatus grow per-type branches, which negates half the benefit of unifying the record model.

## Option 3 — configurable names, standardized *shape*

This is what I'd actually propose. Each writ type declares its own lifecycle, *but* the lifecycle config speaks a standard meta-vocabulary that cross-cutting infrastructure can operate on.

```yaml
writType:
  mandate:
    lifecycle:
      new:        { classification: initial }
      open:       { classification: active }
      stuck:      { classification: active, attrs: [blocking] }
      completed:  { classification: terminal, attrs: [success] }
      failed:     { classification: terminal, attrs: [failure] }
      cancelled:  { classification: terminal, attrs: [neutral] }
    dispatchable: true
  
  capability:
    lifecycle:
      proposed:   { classification: initial }
      ratified:   { classification: active }
      shipped:    { classification: terminal, attrs: [success] }
      retired:    { classification: terminal, attrs: [neutral] }
    dispatchable: false
  
  click:
    lifecycle:
      live:       { classification: active }
      parked:     { classification: active, attrs: [deferred] }
      concluded:  { classification: terminal, attrs: [success] }
      dropped:    { classification: terminal, attrs: [failure] }
    dispatchable: false
```

Two layers:
- **Names** (type-specific, semantically honest) — `stuck`, `ratified`, `parked`.
- **Classification + attrs** (shared, machine-readable) — `initial/active/terminal`, plus tagged attributes like `blocking`, `success`, `deferred`.

Cross-cutting infrastructure operates on the classification layer. The Reckoner's drain detector becomes "count of writs with `classification: active` where `dispatchable: true`, plus active rigs." Generic, still correct, no per-type branches.

Per-type logic operates on the names. Mandate-specific handlers use `stuck`. Capability-specific handlers use `ratified`. No cross-pollination.

This is the well-trodden workflow-engine pattern (Temporal, K8s custom resources, etc.) — every modern state-machine engine lands on some variant of "type-specific states + standard meta-states." Well-lit path.

**Additional affordances worth building into the config:**

- **`dispatchable`** (per-type) — whether this writ type can have rigs attached. Mandate yes; capability, click, product no. This generalizes the current implicit assumption that every writ is dispatchable.
- **`allowedTransitions`** (per-state) — explicit state graph, enforced by Clerk. Prevents ad-hoc transitions and makes the lifecycle documentable.
- **`parentTypes`** (per-type) — what types can be the parent of this type. Capability under product; outcome under capability; mandate under outcome. Prevents structurally invalid trees.
- **`children`-behavior** (per-type) — when children finish, does this writ auto-advance? Mandate has cascade rules today; capability might want "shipped when all child outcomes shipped," or might want manual ratification. Per-type.

## Implications worth calling out

1. **Plugin contract widens.** Observers that subscribe to writ transitions need to either accept all types or declare what they care about. Most will want type-filtering. Lattice pulses already carry source + triggerType; Stacks watchers could grow a type filter. Not a huge lift.

2. **Clerk apparatus grows.** It becomes a generic state-machine + tree engine with per-type config. The mandate-specific quirks (cascade, retry, spider status) become mandate-type concerns, not Clerk concerns. This is a real but bounded refactor.

3. **Ratchet/clicks gets interesting.** If writs are fully configurable, Ratchet's reason-for-existing weakens. You could fold clicks in as a writ type and retire Ratchet-as-separate-apparatus, keeping just the CLI and UI conventions. I wouldn't do this in the same change — it's a follow-on — but the option opens up, and that's a useful signal that the refactor is in the right direction.

4. **Existing first-class-record candidates fold in.** Product, capability, outcome, portfolio — all become writ types with their own lifecycles. The ladder I was sketching becomes a writ tree with type constraints (product → capability → outcome → mandate).

5. **The Reckoner's pulses become more uniform.** Instead of "mandate stuck," it's "any `dispatchable: true` writ hit a state with the `blocking` attr on its parent tree." That's maybe more general than we want right now, but the machinery is there.

## Where I'd push back on myself

The "make everything a writ" direction is seductive because it cleans up a current pain point, but it also risks making writs the kitchen-drawer of the data model. Worth explicitly asking: what *shouldn't* be a writ under this scheme? My first-pass answer: transient/ephemeral things (sessions, pulses, tool calls) stay separate because they don't have meaningful lifecycles or tree structure. Everything that has a state and a parent probably wants to be a writ. That feels principled enough to hold the line.

## Questions I'd want you to answer before committing

1. **Appetite for the Clerk refactor?** This is mid-sized — not a weekend, not a month. Real design work + migration + plugin contract review.
2. **Ratchet/clicks** — pull into this change, defer, or leave permanently separate? I'd defer.
3. **State transition enforcement** — strict (declared transitions only, Clerk rejects illegal ones) or permissive (any-to-any, types that care enforce themselves)? I'd do strict; enforcement is a feature.
4. **Migration strategy for cross-cutting consumers** — when the Reckoner's drain logic changes from "phase-based" to "classification-based," do we cut over everything at once or let old consumers coexist with new? Probably the former given how young everything is.
5. **Do we conclude** `c-mo1mqp0q` (the "evaluate work-tracking primitives beyond writs+clicks" click) in favor of this direction? My read: yes — this *is* the answer to that question. Writs generalized + clicks eventually folded in = we don't need a third primitive.

My recommendation: **Option 3.** Per-type configurable lifecycles, with a standardized classification/attr layer underneath. It's the option that actually addresses the "we keep building new things" pattern rather than deferring it.

message.content: to you other questsion... (4) correct; (5) yes, i say dogfood; (6) agree with defer-but-allow-for-it

content.text:
Got it — logging those.

## Running tally of settled calls

From this conversation so far:

- **Products/portfolio** are first-class; codexes are implementation detail.
- Relationship is **M:N between products and codexes**.
- **One patron, all visions** — client-proxy stays patron-private; guild sees one voice.
- **Decomposition ladder is real** — vision → capabilities → outcomes → commissions. Reckoner must not shortcut vision straight to dispatchable work.
- **Nexus is a product** — dogfooded through this machinery, not a special case.
- **Cross-product dependencies deferred**, but data model leaves room (typed edges later).
- **Writs get Option 3 treatment** (configurable per-type lifecycles, standardized classification layer). Product, capability, outcome, portfolio all become writ types. Clicks eventually fold in; not in this change.

## Still open

From the vision-keeper side:
- **Decomposition ownership** — fully patron-authored for v0, or some Reckoner-proposes-patron-ratifies from day one?
- **Priority storage** — I recommended vector over scalar; needs your sign-off.

From the writ-generalization side:
- **Appetite for the Clerk refactor** as a near-term item? Mid-sized, non-breaking-if-additive, but real design + migration work.
- **State transition enforcement** — strict (declared transitions only, Clerk rejects illegal) vs permissive. I recommended strict.
- **Migration cutover** — one-shot vs coexist. I'd say one-shot given everything's young.
- **Conclude `c-mo1mqp0q`** ("evaluate work-tracking primitives beyond writs+clicks") in favor of this direction?

And one I don't think I asked cleanly yet:
- **Nexus's vision artifact** — if we're dogfooding, someone writes the Nexus product vision + initial capabilities. Is that patron-authored in one sitting, or an iterated elicitation process? This is the first real test of the machinery and sets the pattern.

Ready when you are.

I think the config should include: allowedTransitions, but NOT 'dispatchable' (who knows what ppl might want to dispatch later). I'm open to parentTypes, but also leery. content.text: On it — creating clicks now, then answering the design questions. Let me peek at the clicks skill quickly so I use the right incantations.

content.content:
1	---
2	description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
3	---
4	
5	# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow
6	
7	> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.
8	
9	A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.
10	
11	Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:
12	
13	- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
14	- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
15	- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
16	- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
17	- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.
18	
19	## When to open a click
20	
21	Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:
22	
23	- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
24	- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
25	- A decision is deferred pending more thought
26	- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion
27	
28	Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.
29	
30	## Creating a click
31	
32	    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]
33	
34	- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
35	- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
36	- The click starts in `live` status.
37	
38	### Vocabulary discovery habit
39	
40	When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.
41	
42	For historical references, consult the companion `docs/future/vocabulary-aliases.yaml` — a structured registry mapping legacy terms (Overseer, BA/PO, pre-2026-04-18 "Brief", etc.) to their canonical successors. When old clicks or commits use an unfamiliar term, the registry tells you what it's called now and whether the concept was renamed, subsumed, or dropped.
43	
44	## Viewing clicks
45	
46	Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:
47	
48	**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:
49	
50	    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]
51	
52	Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
53	```
54	● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
55	  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
56	  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
57	  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
58	```
59	Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.
60	
61	**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:
62	
63	    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]
64	
65	One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.
66	
67	**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.
68	
69	**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):
70	
71	    nsg click show <id>
72	
73	**List with filters:**
74	
75	    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]
76	
77	## Status transitions
78	
79	```
80	live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
81	parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
82	live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
83	live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
84	parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
85	parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
86	```
87	
88	Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed. When a concluded decision needs updating (new information, downstream consequences, or the original conclusion turned out wrong), open a new click and link it `supersedes` to the original — see the Links section below.
89	
90	**Park** a click (live -> parked):
91	
92	    nsg click park --id <id>
93	
94	**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):
95	
96	    nsg click resume --id <id>
97	
98	**Conclude** a click with a decision:
99	
100	    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."
101	
102	**Drop** a click without a decision:
103	
104	    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."
105	
106	### Conclusions are tight
107	
108	A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.
109	
110	- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
111	- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
112	- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.
113	
114	Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.
115	
116	## Sub-questions (children)
117	
118	When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:
119	
120	    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>
121	
122	Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").
123	
124	### Enumerated decisions → enumerated children
125	
126	When the analysis inside a click naturally enumerates N items with per-item evaluations (N sources → include/exclude each, N options → pick-one across them, N candidates → accept/reject each), those evaluations are latent sub-clicks. Lift them into children rather than collapsing them into the parent's conclusion.
127	
128	**Recognition trigger:** if you catch yourself writing a conclusion that contains a per-item list with individual rationale ("source A: yes because X; source B: no because Y; …"), stop — that list is telling you the decisions want their own clicks. The parent's conclusion should name the *principle* or *summary*; each child's conclusion carries one item's rationale.
129	
130	This aligns with conclusion-discipline (keeps each conclusion tight, no inline tables) and with capture-liberally (the tree is cheap; per-item decisions are durable structural references worth having).
131	
132	## Links
133	
134	Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):
135	
136	    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>
137	
138	Link types:
139	- `related` — lateral cross-reference
140	- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
141	- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
142	- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved
143	
144	**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.
145	
146	**Supersedes is the canonical post-conclusion correction pattern.** Terminal clicks are immutable — you cannot edit a concluded click's goal or rewrite its conclusion. When the record needs updating, open a new click with the updated framing and link it `supersedes` to the original. The original stays frozen (the historical record of what was decided then doesn't change), and the new click carries the updated inquiry and, once concluded, the updated decision.
147	
148	    nsg click create --goal "<updated framing>"
149	    nsg click link --source-id <new-click> --target-id <old-click> --link-type supersedes
150	    nsg click conclude --id <new-click> --conclusion "<updated decision>"
151	
152	Reach for `supersedes` when a concluded decision turned out wrong or incomplete, when new information materially changes what the answer would have been, or when a downstream consequence forces a retroactive re-think. Do **not** reach for it to rephrase a live click (that's `nsg click amend --goal`), for vocabulary drift (consult the alias registry at `docs/future/vocabulary-aliases.yaml`), for lateral cross-references or branching explorations (use `related`), or for dependency tracking (use `depends-on`).
153	
154	Parent the new click where the inquiry naturally belongs — in the original's subtree if the work is tightly related, or under a different umbrella if the new context has drifted. The `supersedes` link carries the trail regardless of parentage.
155	
156	**Findability gap.** `nsg click tree` and `extract` do not currently surface inbound supersedes links — a reader arriving at an old concluded click will not see that something has superseded it without running `nsg click show` on that click to inspect its links. Until that gap closes, the convention is to name the old click id in the new click's conclusion so the backlink is captured in prose at minimum.
157	
158	Remove a link:
159	
160	    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>
161	
162	## Reparenting
163	
164	Move a click to a new parent or to root:
165	
166	    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
167	    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root
168	
169	Circular parentage is detected and rejected.
170	
171	## Short ID support
172	
173	All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.
174	
175	## Clicks and session continuity
176	
177	Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:
178	
179	- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
180	- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
181	- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.
182	
183	Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.
184

---
description: Work with clicks — Coco's session-continuity mechanism. Invoke when opening, updating, resuming, or concluding a line of inquiry that should outlive the current session.
---

# Clicks — Session-Continuity Workflow

> NOTE: The environment should have a properly defined `GUILD_ROOT` allowing the `nsg` command to be used without `--guild-root`. If this is not the case, the guild root (at `/workspace/vibers`) can be appended to commands as needed.

A **click** is an atomic decision-node managed by the Ratchet apparatus. Each click captures one question or inquiry; when resolved, it records the conclusion. Clicks are organized in a tree — children are sub-questions of their parent. Together, the click tree forms a structured record of the reasoning and decisions that guide the guild's work.

Clicks are Coco's primary mechanism for session continuity, replacing the earlier quest writ type. Key differences from quests:

- **Goal is immutable.** Set at creation, never changes. If the framing is wrong, drop and create a new one.
- **Conclusion is write-once.** Set by `conclude` or `drop`, then frozen.
- **No body files.** Click goals are short (one sentence). Long-form exploration lives in session transcripts, joinable via session ID.
- **The tree is the product.** Value lives in structure (hierarchy, decomposition), not prose. Children are the todo list.
- **Four statuses:** `live | parked | concluded | dropped`. Simple, designed for inquiry.

## When to open a click

Open a click when a conversation or line of thought has enough substance to outlive the current session. Heuristics:

- Sean is exploring a design question across multiple turns
- You've tabled something with "let's come back to this"
- A decision is deferred pending more thought
- A line of inquiry has its own arc — opening question, accumulating sub-questions, eventual conclusion

Don't open a click for every exchange. One-off questions, quick clarifications, and routine operational work don't need one.

## Creating a click

    nsg click create --goal "How should we handle X?" [--parent-id <id>]

- The `--goal` is the question or inquiry — frame it as a question when possible.
- Use `--parent-id` to nest under an existing click (sub-question decomposition).
- The click starts in `live` status.

### Vocabulary discovery habit

When opening any new click, scan `docs/future/guild-vocabulary.md` for related terms and consider creating cross-links. The vocabulary tome holds latent metaphor concepts that imply future features; the only mechanism that surfaces them at the right moment is this manual habit.

For historical references, consult the companion `docs/future/vocabulary-aliases.yaml` — a structured registry mapping legacy terms (Overseer, BA/PO, pre-2026-04-18 "Brief", etc.) to their canonical successors. When old clicks or commits use an unfamiliar term, the registry tells you what it's called now and whether the concept was renamed, subsumed, or dropped.

## Viewing clicks

Two commands do the orientation work. Reach for them in this order:

**1. Tree view** — board-level scan, the first thing you run at startup:

    nsg click tree [--root-id <id>] [--status live] [--depth N]

Renders the click forest with box-drawing connectors and status indicators:
```
● How should the quest system evolve?                    [live]
  ├── ○ Is the friction removable or structural?         [concluded]
  ├── ● What should we call these things?                [live]
  └── ◇ Do quests need an event-log layer?               [parked]
```
Status indicators: `●` live, `◇` parked, `○` concluded, `✕` dropped.

**2. Extract a subtree** — the primary narrative-loading command, used whenever the conversation turns toward a specific area:

    nsg click extract --id <id> [--format md|json]

One call loads the whole subtree — goals, statuses, and conclusions for any concluded/dropped clicks — as a structured document. This is how you orient on any line of inquiry.

**Don't do this:** don't walk a subtree by calling `show` on each child to reconstruct the narrative. That's what `extract` is for. If you find yourself running `show` three or more times in a row to understand the shape of an area, stop and run `extract` on the common ancestor instead.

**Show a single click** — use only for single-click inspection (checking one click's links, parent, status, or conclusion):

    nsg click show <id>

**List with filters:**

    nsg click list [--status live] [--status parked] [--limit 50]

## Status transitions

```
live ──> parked       (park — deliberately dormant, pick up later)
parked ──> live       (resume — back to active exploration)
live ──> concluded    (conclude — decision reached, conclusion required)
live ──> dropped      (drop — abandoned, reason required)
parked ──> concluded  (conclude directly from parked)
parked ──> dropped    (drop directly from parked)
```

Terminal states (concluded, dropped) are immutable — no further changes allowed. When a concluded decision needs updating (new information, downstream consequences, or the original conclusion turned out wrong), open a new click and link it `supersedes` to the original — see the Links section below.

**Park** a click (live -> parked):

    nsg click park --id <id>

**Resume** a parked click (parked -> live):

    nsg click resume --id <id>

**Conclude** a click with a decision:

    nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "We decided to..."

**Drop** a click without a decision:

    nsg click drop --id <id> --conclusion "Moot because..."

### Conclusions are tight

A conclusion is a pointer-note for future-me, not an essay for posterity. Target shape: **decision + one-line reasoning + (optional) one-clause naming the rejected alternatives.** A small paragraph, not a screenful.

- **No markdown structure.** No headers, no tables, no "Recommendation:" sections. If a conclusion needs structure, it's too long.
- **Don't re-argue rejected options.** Name them in a clause ("rejected `spider.sequence` for directional ambiguity"); don't rebuild the tradeoff table.
- **Exploration stays in the session transcript.** The analytical work — enumerated cases, stress tests, option matrices — belongs in chat, joinable via session ID. Only the distilled outcome lands in the conclusion.

Verbose conclusions are the main context cost of clicks. Keep them short and the tree can grow freely.

## Sub-questions (children)

When a click spawns distinct sub-inquiries, create child clicks:

    nsg click create --goal "Sub-question A?" --parent-id <parent-id>

Children are the decomposition mechanism. Resolving a parent typically means resolving or dropping its children. But there's no automatic cascading — a parent can be concluded while children are still live (e.g., "we answered the main question; remaining sub-questions are moot").

### Enumerated decisions → enumerated children

When the analysis inside a click naturally enumerates N items with per-item evaluations (N sources → include/exclude each, N options → pick-one across them, N candidates → accept/reject each), those evaluations are latent sub-clicks. Lift them into children rather than collapsing them into the parent's conclusion.

**Recognition trigger:** if you catch yourself writing a conclusion that contains a per-item list with individual rationale ("source A: yes because X; source B: no because Y; …"), stop — that list is telling you the decisions want their own clicks. The parent's conclusion should name the *principle* or *summary*; each child's conclusion carries one item's rationale.

This aligns with conclusion-discipline (keeps each conclusion tight, no inline tables) and with capture-liberally (the tree is cheap; per-item decisions are durable structural references worth having).

## Links

Typed links connect clicks to each other or to writs (cross-substrate):

    nsg click link --source-id <click-id> --target-id <target-id> --link-type <type>

Link types:
- `related` — lateral cross-reference
- `commissioned` — this click's conclusion produced a commission (target is a writ ID)
- `supersedes` — this click replaces the target
- `depends-on` — this click can't be concluded until the target is resolved

**Commissioned links are automatic.** When a click's conclusion spawns a writ (brief posted, commission dispatched), create the `commissioned` link from the click to the writ id without asking. This preserves the click-to-artifact trail in the tree and is cheap to maintain. If multiple clicks contribute to one commission, link the narrowest conclusion-bearing click; add additional links from contributing parents only if the trail would be lossy otherwise.

**Supersedes is the canonical post-conclusion correction pattern.** Terminal clicks are immutable — you cannot edit a concluded click's goal or rewrite its conclusion. When the record needs updating, open a new click with the updated framing and link it `supersedes` to the original. The original stays frozen (the historical record of what was decided then doesn't change), and the new click carries the updated inquiry and, once concluded, the updated decision.

    nsg click create --goal "<updated framing>"
    nsg click link --source-id <new-click> --target-id <old-click> --link-type supersedes
    nsg click conclude --id <new-click> --conclusion "<updated decision>"

Reach for `supersedes` when a concluded decision turned out wrong or incomplete, when new information materially changes what the answer would have been, or when a downstream consequence forces a retroactive re-think. Do **not** reach for it to rephrase a live click (that's `nsg click amend --goal`), for vocabulary drift (consult the alias registry at `docs/future/vocabulary-aliases.yaml`), for lateral cross-references or branching explorations (use `related`), or for dependency tracking (use `depends-on`).

Parent the new click where the inquiry naturally belongs — in the original's subtree if the work is tightly related, or under a different umbrella if the new context has drifted. The `supersedes` link carries the trail regardless of parentage.

**Findability gap.** `nsg click tree` and `extract` do not currently surface inbound supersedes links — a reader arriving at an old concluded click will not see that something has superseded it without running `nsg click show` on that click to inspect its links. Until that gap closes, the convention is to name the old click id in the new click's conclusion so the backlink is captured in prose at minimum.

Remove a link:

    nsg click unlink --source-id <id> --target-id <id> --link-type <type>

## Reparenting

Move a click to a new parent or to root:

    nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent-id <new-parent-id>
    nsg click reparent --id <id>                               # move to root

Circular parentage is detected and rejected.

## Short ID support

All ID parameters accept short prefixes (e.g., `c-mo1ee` instead of the full ID). The Ratchet resolves prefixes and errors on ambiguity.

## Clicks and session continuity

Clicks provide continuity across sessions. The flow is:

- **Startup** — scan the click tree with `nsg click tree --status live --status parked` to see what's in flight. When the conversation turns toward a specific area, use `nsg click extract --id <id>` to load the full subtree as narrative context. Reserve `nsg click show <id>` for single-click inspection. Don't eagerly read every click.
- **During the session** — open new clicks for substantial inquiries; create child clicks for sub-questions. Conclude or drop clicks as decisions are reached.
- **Wrap-up** — park any live clicks that won't be continued immediately. Conclude clicks where decisions were reached this session. The click tree itself is the session's durable record of what was explored and decided.

Because click goals are immutable and conclusions are write-once, there's no body to maintain or keep in sync. The session transcript (joinable via session ID) holds the full exploration context. Clicks are just the structural skeleton — questions asked and answers given.
